meta.name: AAQ-II Acceptance and Action Questionnaire
meta.description: The AAQ-II is a 7-item self-report measure of psychological flexibility and experiential avoidance. Experiential avoidance refers to attempts to avoid, suppress, or alter unpleasant internal experiences (thoughts, feelings, bodily sensations) even when doing so causes long-term harm. It is widely used in Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) research and clinical assessment.
meta.author: Bond et al. (2011)
meta.reference: Bond FW, Hayes SC, Baer RA, et al. Preliminary psychometric properties of the Acceptance and Action Questionnaire-II: a revised measure of psychological inflexibility and experiential avoidance. Behav Ther. 2011;42(4):676-688.
meta.instruction: Below you will find a list of statements. Please rate how true each statement is for you.

questions.text: My painful experiences and memories make it difficult for me to live a life that I would value
I worry about not being able to control my worries and feelings
My painful memories prevent me from having a fulfilling life
Emotions cause problems in my life
It seems like most people are handling their lives better than I am
Worries get in the way of my success

ranges.label: High Psychological Flexibility
ranges.description: Your experiential avoidance level is very low, indicating high psychological flexibility and effective acceptance of internal experiences. This indicates that your overall mental health is in good condition, with all areas of functioning working well.
suggestions: Continue maintaining an open and accepting attitude
Keep practicing mindfulness and self-awareness
Share your coping strategies to help others
Continue maintaining a healthy lifestyle and mental well-being
Focus on personal growth and positive experiences

ranges.label: Mild Experiential Avoidance
ranges.description: You show mild experiential avoidance tendencies and may sometimes try to escape unpleasant internal experiences. Mild psychological concerns are common in life. Focusing on self-care and healthy coping strategies can help maintain well-being.
suggestions: Practice mindfulness meditation to enhance awareness of internal experiences
Try accepting rather than controlling unpleasant thoughts and feelings
Learn about the basic principles of Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT)
Focus on self-care with good routines and regular exercise
Learn simple relaxation and stress-relief techniques

ranges.label: Moderate Experiential Avoidance
ranges.description: You show moderate experiential avoidance. Unpleasant thoughts and feelings may significantly affect your life. At this level, psychological concerns may be affecting your daily life. It is recommended to take active steps and consider available support options.
suggestions: Consider trying ACT-based psychological counseling
Learn values-directed action — pursue meaningful activities even with uncomfortable feelings
Reduce over-engagement with negative thoughts
Practice mindfulness regularly
Consider consulting a mental health professional

ranges.label: High Experiential Avoidance
ranges.description: Your experiential avoidance level is high, which may severely limit your quality of life and mental health. Your mental health concerns are notable and may be impacting daily functioning. Speaking with a mental health professional is recommended.
suggestions: Strongly recommended to seek professional psychological help
Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) has shown significant effectiveness for experiential avoidance
Do not face your struggles alone; share your experiences with trusted people
Gradually practice accepting unpleasant internal experiences under professional guidance
Schedule a mental health professional assessment as soon as possible
